title: Dog Crusty Build-Up on Penis Tip: Triage and Care Guide
meta_desc: Learn about crusty buildup on your dog's penis, why it happens, and when you should consult a veterinarian for this common issue.
content_html:
What is this crusty buildup?

Finding a yellowish-white or slightly green crusty buildup on your dog's penis or sheath is actually quite common. This substance is usually smegma, a natural mixture of dead skin cells, oils, and moisture that serves as a lubricant and protective barrier. When it dries at the tip of the prepuce, it forms a crust. While often normal, an increase in this discharge can sometimes indicate balanoposthitis, which is a mild inflammation of the area.

Is it an emergency?

Urgency level: Low. In the vast majority of cases, crusty buildup on a dog's penis is not a medical emergency. If your dog is acting normally, eating well, and urinating without discomfort, you can likely monitor the situation at home. However, if you notice signs of pain, blood, significant swelling, or if your dog is constantly licking the area, you should schedule a routine check-up with your veterinarian.

Why a photo helps triage

Providing a clear photo to a veterinary professional can be incredibly helpful. Because many different conditions can look similar, a photo allows a vet to see the color, texture, and exact location of the buildup. This helps them determine if the discharge is within the normal range for your specific dog or if it looks like a sign of an infection or a growth that requires more immediate intervention.

faqs.question: Should I try to clean the crusty area?
faqs.answer: Yes, you can gently clean the tip with a soft cloth and warm water. Avoid using harsh soaps or alcohol, as the tissue in that area is very sensitive.

faqs.question: Can I use over-the-counter ointments?
faqs.answer: It is best to avoid applying any creams or ointments until a veterinarian has examined the area, as some human products can be toxic if licked off.

faqs.question: What are the warning signs of a more serious issue?
faqs.answer: If you see blood, a foul odor, difficulty urinating, or if the dog seems lethargic, these are signs that you should contact your vet sooner rather than later.
